diagrams.title: Identifiers vs Keywords vs Literals Overview
diagrams.description: A three-column comparison diagram showing the fundamental differences between identifiers (names for entities), keywords (reserved words), and literals (fixed values) in Python. Each column contains examples to clarify the distinctions.
elements: IDENTIFIERS column with examples: variable names (age, student_name), function names (calculate_total), class names (Person, Car)
KEYWORDS column with Python reserved words: if, else, while, for, class, def, import, return, True, False, None
LITERALS column with examples: numeric (42, 3.14), string ('hello'), boolean (True, False), None
Visual distinction showing identifiers are user-defined while keywords and literals are language-defined
diagrams.purpose: Establish the foundational understanding that identifiers are names we create, keywords are reserved by Python, and literals are the actual data values we work with in OOP

diagrams.title: Python Identifier Naming Rules and Conventions
diagrams.description: A diagram illustrating the rules for valid identifiers in Python and the naming conventions used in OOP, showing correct and incorrect examples for variables, functions, and classes.
elements: Rules section: must start with letter or underscore, can contain letters, numbers, underscores, case-sensitive, cannot use keywords
Valid examples: my_var, _private, ClassName, calculate_sum, PI_CONSTANT
Invalid examples shown with X mark: 2variable (starts with number), my-var (hyphen), class (keyword)
Convention section: snake_case for variables/functions, PascalCase for classes, UPPER_CASE for constants
diagrams.purpose: Teach proper identifier usage which is essential for writing clean, readable object-oriented Python code with proper encapsulation and naming standards

diagrams.title: Python Literal Types in OOP Context
diagrams.description: A hierarchical diagram showing all types of literals in Python and how they are used when creating objects and defining class attributes in OOP programming.
elements: String literals: single quotes, double quotes, triple quotes for multiline
None literal: representing absence of value
Collection literals: lists [1,2,3], dicts {'key': 'value'}, tuples (1,2), sets {1,2,3}
OOP usage examples: class attribute assignments, default parameter values, dictionary for class data
diagrams.purpose: Help learners understand all literal types as they are fundamental to initializing object attributes, defining constants, and working with data structures in Python classes
